advantages: Standard sklearn Transformers compose cleanly in Pipeline and GridSearchCV—one fit path prevents vocabulary leakage across folds.
Sparse bag-of-words matrices scale to large corpora on CPU with interpretable token-level features via get_feature_names_out().
CountVectorizer and TfidfVectorizer require no GPU and train in a single pass over text—strong baselines before neural encoders.
HashingVectorizer bounds memory for open-vocabulary or streaming text when storing every token is impractical.
disadvantages: Bag-of-words vectorizers discard word order and context—"not bad" and "not good" look similar without n-grams or better encoders.
Large vocabularies produce high-dimensional sparse matrices; naive max_features tuning can drop rare but informative tokens.
HashingVectorizer introduces collision noise and offers no inverse mapping from columns back to exact tokens.
Extractor choice must match downstream model assumptions—negative TF–IDF weights break count-based generative models.
